pois.commentary: Every Sunday morning the Hawke's Bay Showgrounds in Hastings host one of New Zealand's best farmers' markets. Over 50 producers sell everything from stone fruit and artisan cheese to fresh bread, smoked meats and local olive oil. The coffee stalls are excellent, and there's usually live music. Arrive by 8:30am to beat the crowds and get the best stone fruit in season.

reviews.summary: Consistently rated among New Zealand's top farmers' markets. The variety and quality of produce impresses. Can get very busy by 10am in summer — early arrival recommended.

pois.name: Hawthorne Coffee Roasters
pois.short: Napier's best specialty coffee roaster — single-origin beans, expert baristas and excellent pastries.
pois.commentary: Hawthorne has elevated Napier's coffee game with carefully sourced single-origin beans roasted on-site. The café is a clean, modern space in the Ahuriri precinct where you can watch the roasting process while sipping what many locals call the best flat white in the Bay. The pastries and cabinet food are a step above the usual café fare. A morning ritual for Napier's coffee aficionados.

reviews.summary: Coffee lovers praise the single-origin quality and knowledgeable baristas. The pastries get special mentions. Limited seating can mean waits on busy mornings.

pois.name: Brave Brewing Co
pois.short: Hastings craft brewery with a buzzing taproom — rotating taps, wood-fired pizzas and live music.
pois.commentary: In a region dominated by wine, Brave Brewing has carved out a loyal following with hop-forward IPAs, sessionable lagers and experimental brews. The taproom in Hastings is a lively gathering spot with wood-fired pizzas, regular live music and a rotating selection of guest taps from other NZ craft breweries. A great alternative for the non-wine-drinker in your group.

reviews.summary: Beer lovers appreciate having a quality craft option in wine country. The taproom atmosphere is fun and lively. Some note the pizza quality varies, but the beer is consistently good.

pois.name: Trinity Hill Winery
pois.short: Gimblett Gravels flagship producer — powerful Syrah and Bordeaux blends from warm river gravels.
pois.commentary: Trinity Hill's vineyard sits on the famous Gimblett Gravels — warm, stony river terraces that produce some of New Zealand's most concentrated red wines. The Homage Syrah is their pinnacle, a powerful, complex wine that ages beautifully. The cellar door tastings progress through the range from approachable to premium, and the staff are excellent at explaining why Gimblett Gravels matter.

reviews.summary: The Gimblett Gravels reds are world-class. The Homage Syrah is regularly called one of NZ's best. The cellar door experience is informative and professional.

pois.name: Smith & Sheth
pois.short: Master of Wine Steve Smith's urban tasting room in Ahuriri — single-site wines from across NZ.
pois.commentary: Steve Smith MW (one of just 400 Masters of Wine in the world) and Brian Sheth created a winery focused on single-site expressions from New Zealand's best terroirs. The Ahuriri tasting room in Napier is a stylish industrial space where you can taste wines from Hawke's Bay, Marlborough and Central Otago side by side. The education alone is worth the visit — few people know NZ wine better than Steve.

reviews.summary: Wine geeks love the Master of Wine perspective. The single-site concept is educational. The urban tasting room can feel less special than a vineyard visit.

pois.name: Bistronomy
pois.short: French-NZ fusion in Napier's art deco precinct — seasonal tasting menus with Bay wines.
pois.commentary: Bistronomy brings French technique to Hawke's Bay produce in a charming Napier setting. The seasonal tasting menus are expertly constructed, with local lamb, seafood and seasonal vegetables given a French twist. The wine list highlights Hawke's Bay producers, and the art deco neighbourhood adds atmosphere. Lunch is excellent value; dinner is a more extended affair. Booking recommended.

reviews.summary: The French-NZ fusion works beautifully. The lunch menu is excellent value. Some find the space small, which can feel crowded on busy evenings.

pois.name: The Common Room
pois.short: Havelock North's best coffee — specialty roasters with a community-focused neighbourhood café.
pois.commentary: The Common Room has become the caffeine hub of Havelock North's wine village, serving expertly prepared single-origin coffees in a warm, neighbourhood setting. The cabinet food is a step above standard café fare, and the pastries are made in-house. It's where winemakers start their mornings and visitors fuel up before cellar door visits. The flat white is excellent.

reviews.summary: Havelock North's best coffee according to locals. The neighbourhood atmosphere is welcoming. Small space means limited seating at peak times.

pois.name: Hawke's Bay Wine Trail (by bike)
pois.short: 200km of dedicated cycle trails linking Napier, Hastings and 30+ cellar doors — NZ's best wine-by-bike experience.
pois.commentary: The Hawke's Bay Trails network is the best way to experience the region's wine country without worrying about drink-driving. Over 200km of dedicated cycle paths link Napier, Hastings and the surrounding wine areas, passing 30+ cellar doors. E-bike hire makes the gentle terrain effortless, and numerous operators offer guided wine-by-bike tours. The Bridge Pa Triangle and Gimblett Gravels loops are the wine highlights.

reviews.summary: The best way to explore Hawke's Bay wine country. E-bikes make it accessible for everyone. Some trails are on-road sections which can feel less safe.

pois.name: Crab Farm Winery
pois.short: Boutique Hawke's Bay winery with a popular café and cellar door — family-friendly with great Viognier.
pois.commentary: Crab Farm Winery (named after the neighbouring crab research station, not actual crabs) has carved out a loyal local following with its friendly cellar door and café. The Viognier and Chardonnay are excellent, and the café serves brunch and lunch with generous portions. The garden is family-friendly, and the art exhibitions add an extra dimension. A less touristy alternative to the big-name Hawke's Bay wineries.

reviews.summary: Friendly and approachable. The café food is generous. The Viognier surprises visitors who expect only reds from Hawke's Bay.

pois.name: Abbey Estate Winery
pois.short: Small Havelock North boutique winery — personal tastings with the winemaker and limited-release wines.
pois.commentary: Abbey Estate is one of Havelock North's smaller wineries, where the tasting experience is genuinely personal — you'll often be served by the winemaker. The Merlot and Syrah from their Havelock North vineyard are concentrated and complex, and the limited production means the wines often sell out at the cellar door. It's the antidote to the bigger, more commercial operations nearby.

reviews.summary: The personal touch is the highlight. The wines are limited and worth seeking out. Small operation means restricted opening hours.

pois.name: Gimblett Gravels Wine District
pois.short: Hawke's Bay's premier wine sub-region — warm river gravels producing NZ's best Syrah and Bordeaux-style reds.
pois.commentary: The Gimblett Gravels is a 800-hectare area of warm, stony river terraces that was nearly quarried for gravel before visionary winemakers realised its potential. Today it's arguably New Zealand's most important red wine district. The warm stones radiate heat, ripening Syrah, Cabernet and Merlot to perfection. Craggy Range, Trinity Hill, Villa Maria and Te Awa all have Gimblett Gravels cellar doors. A must-visit for red wine lovers.

reviews.summary: NZ's best red wine district. The terroir story is fascinating. Multiple cellar doors make it easy to spend a full day here.

pois.name: Cape Kidnappers Gannet Colony
pois.short: The world's largest accessible mainland gannet colony — 20,000 birds nesting on dramatic coastal cliffs.
pois.commentary: Cape Kidnappers hosts one of the world's largest and most accessible mainland gannet colonies, with around 20,000 birds in residence from July to April. You can reach the colony via a spectacular coastal walk at low tide or by tractor-trailer tour. The birds are remarkably unperturbed by visitors.

reviews.summary: Bucket-list wildlife experience. The tractor tour is fun but the walk is more rewarding. Check tides carefully.

pois.name: Boundary Stream Mainland Island
pois.short: DOC mainland island with intensive pest control — kiwi, kōkako, and robin thrive in regenerating bush.
pois.commentary: Boundary Stream Scenic Reserve is one of DOC's six mainland island sites. Intensive predator control has allowed kiwi, kōkako, North Island robin, and whiteheads to flourish. Multiple walking tracks ranging from 30 minutes to full-day offer excellent birding.

reviews.summary: Exceptional birding in beautiful bush. The robins are incredibly tame. Multiple loop track options.

pois.name: Waitangi Regional Park Wetland
pois.short: Restored estuary wetland near Napier — royal spoonbill, bittern, and seasonal wading birds.
pois.commentary: The Waitangi Estuary at the mouth of the Ngaruroro River has been partially restored as a regional park. Royal spoonbill, white-faced heron, pied stilt, and Australasian bittern frequent the wetlands. A boardwalk provides easy viewing access.

reviews.summary: Good birding close to Napier. The boardwalk is well maintained. Best at low tide.

pois.name: Te Angiangi Marine Reserve
pois.short: Remote rocky reef marine reserve on the southern Hawke's Bay coast — crayfish, paua, and reef fish.
pois.commentary: Te Angiangi Marine Reserve protects 446 hectares of rocky reef and sandy seabed near Aramoana. The reserve is remote and uncrowded, with excellent rock pool exploring and snorkelling when conditions allow. Crayfish and paua numbers are noticeably higher than surrounding areas.

reviews.summary: Off the beaten track but rewarding. Best in calm conditions. Rock pools are excellent at low tide.

pois.name: Pekapeka Swamp — Wetland Restoration
pois.short: Major wetland restoration project on the Heretaunga Plains — spotless crake and Australasian bittern habitat.
pois.commentary: Pekapeka Swamp is one of the last remnants of the vast wetlands that once covered the Heretaunga Plains. Restoration efforts are underway to enhance habitat for nationally threatened species including Australasian bittern, spotless crake, and fernbird.

reviews.summary: Important restoration project. Bird viewing requires patience and binoculars. Best at dawn.

pois.name: Gannet Beach Adventures — Clifton
pois.short: Tractor-trailer rides along the beach to Cape Kidnappers gannet colony — a unique NZ wildlife tour.
pois.commentary: Gannet Beach Adventures runs tractor-trailer tours from Clifton along the beach to the Cape Kidnappers gannet colony. The ride itself is an experience, passing beneath eroding clay cliffs with visible geological layers millions of years old. Booking essential in peak season.

reviews.summary: Great fun for all ages. The tractor ride is half the experience. Book ahead in summer.

pois.name: Opouahi Scenic Reserve — Bush Birds
pois.short: Remnant lowland podocarp forest with kaka, kererū, and rifleman in the Hawke's Bay hinterland.
pois.commentary: Opouahi Scenic Reserve protects a rare remnant of lowland podocarp forest in the Maungaharuru Range foothills. The forest is home to kaka, kererū, rifleman, and long-tailed cuckoo. A short loop track passes through impressive rimu and matai trees.

reviews.summary: Lovely bush walk with good bird variety. The old-growth trees are impressive. Track is well maintained.

pois.name: Lake Tūtira — Wetland Birds
pois.short: Scenic lake with excellent waterbird viewing — black swan, dabchick, and NZ scaup on calm waters.
pois.commentary: Lake Tūtira, made famous by Herbert Guthrie-Smith's book Tutira, is a picturesque lake surrounded by regenerating bush. Black swan, NZ dabchick, grey teal, NZ scaup, and shag species are easily observed from the shore and walking tracks around the lake.

reviews.summary: Beautiful lake with good bird variety. The loop walk is pleasant. Historical significance adds interest.

pois.name: Blowhard Bush Scenic Reserve — Kiwi Habitat
pois.short: Remote bush reserve with intensive kiwi management — North Island brown kiwi calling at night.
pois.commentary: Blowhard Bush in the Kaweka Range foothills is part of DOC's kiwi recovery programme for Hawke's Bay. Intensive stoat trapping protects breeding kiwi. The reserve's remoteness means excellent native bush with minimal human disturbance. Night listening possible from the track edges.

reviews.summary: For serious birders and kiwi enthusiasts. Remote access adds to the experience. Best in breeding season.

pois.name: Ahuriri Estuary — Wading Birds
pois.short: Inner harbour estuary in Napier with excellent shorebird viewing — godwit, pied stilt, and oystercatcher.
pois.commentary: The Ahuriri Estuary in Napier's inner harbour is one of Hawke's Bay's most accessible birding sites. Bar-tailed godwit, pied stilt, variable oystercatcher, and royal spoonbill are regular visitors. The Westshore Wildlife Reserve on the estuary margin provides good viewing.

reviews.summary: Easy access birding in town. Best at low tide. The Westshore end has the best viewing spots.

pois.name: Mahia Peninsula — Blue Penguin Nesting
pois.short: Little blue penguins nest along the rocky Mahia Peninsula coast — watch them come ashore at dusk.
pois.commentary: The Mahia Peninsula's rocky coastline provides nesting habitat for a population of little blue penguins. The Mahia Beach area and rocky outcrops around the peninsula headlands are the best spots. Dusk viewing from a respectful distance during breeding season (Sep-Feb).

reviews.summary: Beautiful remote setting. Penguin viewing requires patience and timing. The peninsula itself is worth exploring.

pois.name: Shine Falls — Boundary Stream Ecology
pois.short: 58-metre waterfall walk through regenerating bush — longfin eels and native fish in the stream below.
pois.commentary: The track to Shine Falls passes through Boundary Stream Mainland Island, offering both a spectacular 58-metre waterfall and excellent ecological interpretation. The stream below the falls holds longfin eels and native fish species. Bush birds are abundant along the track.

reviews.summary: Stunning waterfall with great bush birding on the way. Track is moderate. Combine with Boundary Stream walks.

pois.name: MTG Hawke's Bay Museum Theatre Gallery
pois.short: Napier's museum, theatre, and gallery complex telling the dramatic story of the 1931 earthquake and Art Deco rebuild.
pois.commentary: MTG Hawke's Bay (Museum Theatre Gallery) is Napier's principal cultural institution, located on the Marine Parade waterfront. The museum's permanent exhibition powerfully tells the story of the devastating 1931 Hawke's Bay earthquake that destroyed Napier and the remarkable Art Deco rebuild that followed, creating the unique streetscape the city is famous for today. Displays include earthquake relics, survivor testimonies, a recreated 1930s streetscape, and extensive Art Deco architectural photography. The gallery spaces host rotating exhibitions of regional and national art. The building itself is a modern, award-winning design that sits well alongside Napier's historic waterfront architecture. The century theatre hosts films, performances, and talks. Admission to permanent exhibitions is free. MTG provides essential context for understanding Napier's Art Deco heritage — visit here before taking a walking tour of the town. The café overlooks the waterfront, and the shop stocks excellent local art and design.

reviews.summary: The earthquake exhibition is deeply moving and really brings the 1931 disaster to life. Essential background before exploring Napier's Art Deco streets. Great gallery exhibitions too, and free entry.

pois.name: National Tobacco Company Building
pois.short: Napier's finest Art Deco building with stunning Māori-inspired reliefs and original 1930s interior details.
pois.commentary: The National Tobacco Company Building on Ahuriri's waterfront is widely regarded as the finest Art Deco building in New Zealand and one of the best in the world. Designed by Louis Hay and built in 1933 after the earthquake, the building is a masterpiece of the Art Deco Stripped Classical style, featuring extraordinary exterior reliefs of roses, raupo (bulrushes), and nikau palms executed in a stylised Māori-influenced design. The interior retains original terrazzo floors, pressed zinc ceilings, and beautiful leadlight windows. The building is classified as a Category I historic place and was a key site in Napier's successful Art Deco heritage campaign. Now operated as a commercial venue, the building can be viewed from outside at any time, and interior access may be available during business hours or Art Deco Festival weekends. Located in the Ahuriri waterfront area, it combines well with a walk around this atmospheric harbour precinct.

reviews.summary: Absolutely stunning building — the decorative reliefs are incredible works of art. A must-see for anyone interested in Art Deco architecture. The Ahuriri location adds to the atmosphere.

pois.name: Hastings Municipal Theatre (Toitoi)
pois.short: Beautifully preserved Art Deco theatre in Hastings, now the Toitoi performing arts hub for Hawke's Bay.
pois.commentary: The Hastings Municipal Theatre, now branded as Toitoi — Hawke's Bay Arts & Events Centre, is one of the finest remaining Art Deco theatres in New Zealand. Built in 1915 and extensively remodelled in the Art Deco style after the 1931 earthquake, the theatre features a magnificent interior with decorative plasterwork, period lighting, and elegant proportions. The Opera House auditorium seats approximately 950 and hosts a year-round programme of touring shows, local theatre, music, dance, and comedy. The complex also includes the intimate Playhouse Studio for smaller productions. Toitoi serves as Hawke's Bay's premier performing arts centre, complementing Napier's Municipal Theatre across the twin cities. The Art Deco architectural details throughout the building reward close inspection. Check the programme for current shows — the venue attracts quality touring productions. Located on Hastings Street in the centre of town, surrounded by more examples of Art Deco commercial architecture.

reviews.summary: A beautiful Art Deco theatre with real character and charm. The interior is gorgeous and seeing a show here feels special. Great programme of events throughout the year.

pois.name: Ocean Beach Hawke's Bay
pois.short: Wild and dramatic surf beach south of Cape Kidnappers with powerful waves and remote, untamed beauty.
pois.commentary: Ocean Beach is a spectacular wild beach south of Cape Kidnappers on the Hawke's Bay coast, offering a dramatically different experience from the region's gentler harbour beaches. The beach stretches for several kilometres beneath imposing sandstone cliffs, exposed to powerful Pacific swells that create excellent surfing conditions for experienced riders. The drive to Ocean Beach passes through fertile farmland before descending steeply to the coast. The beach is not patrolled, and strong rips and currents mean swimming requires caution and experience. The scenic beauty is outstanding — golden sand, dramatic cliff formations, and crashing surf create a wild and invigorating atmosphere. The Cape Kidnappers gannet colony is accessible by walking along the beach at low tide (check tide times carefully). A surf lifesaving club operates during summer weekends. Ocean Beach is popular with locals but rarely crowded. Take care on the steep, winding access road.

reviews.summary: A breathtakingly wild and beautiful beach — quite unlike anything else in Hawke's Bay. The cliffs and surf are dramatic. Be careful of currents and check tides if walking to the gannets.

pois.name: Waimarama Beach
pois.short: Beautiful golden sand beach south of Hastings with good surf, safe swimming, and a classic Kiwi beach feel.
pois.commentary: Waimarama Beach is one of Hawke's Bay's most popular swimming and surfing beaches, a beautiful crescent of golden sand about 25 kilometres south of Hastings. The beach faces east into the Pacific and receives reliable swell, making the northern end popular with surfers, while the sheltered southern end is better for swimming. Surf lifeguards patrol during peak summer weekends and holidays. The small beachside settlement maintains a relaxed, uncommercialised character with a general store, camp ground, and community surf club. Bare Island (Te Karahumuhumu) sits just offshore, an impressive rock formation accessible on foot at very low tides. The beach is backed by rolling farmland and the scenic drive from Hastings through the rural hills is part of the experience. Waimarama captures the essence of the classic New Zealand beach holiday — uncrowded, beautiful, and unpretentious. Popular with families and surfers alike.

reviews.summary: A genuinely lovely beach with great surf and a real Kiwi holiday feel. Bare Island is impressive and the drive out is beautiful. One of Hawke's Bay's best-kept secrets.

pois.name: Mission Estate Winery
pois.short: New Zealand's oldest winery founded by French missionaries in 1851, with a stunning seminary building and restaurant.
pois.commentary: Mission Estate is New Zealand's oldest winery, founded by Marist missionaries from France in 1851. The imposing white seminary building, dating from 1880, sits on a hillside above the Taradale vineyards with sweeping views across the Heretaunga Plains to the coast. The cellar door offers tastings of the full range, from the accessible estate wines to the premium Jewelstone and Huchet single-vineyard labels. The on-site restaurant is set in an elegant dining room within the seminary, serving cuisine designed to complement the wines. The annual Mission Concert on the front lawn attracts major international artists and is one of New Zealand's premier outdoor music events. The history of French missionaries establishing viticulture in Hawke's Bay adds a fascinating layer to the tasting experience.

reviews.summary: The historic seminary setting is breathtaking and the wines are excellent across the range. The restaurant offers a memorable dining experience with vineyard views. A quintessential Hawke's Bay experience.

pois.name: Napier-Taupo Road Scenic Drive
pois.short: Dramatic highway crossing the Kaweka and Ahimanawa ranges between Hawke's Bay and the Central Plateau.
pois.commentary: The Napier-Taupo Road (SH5) is one of the North Island's most dramatic highway drives, climbing from the coastal plains of Hawke's Bay over the rugged Kaweka and Ahimanawa ranges to the volcanic Central Plateau. The 148km route follows the Esk Valley before ascending through native bush and farmland to the Titiokura Summit at 678 metres, where a lookout provides spectacular views back to the coast and Hawke's Bay. The road continues through the Rangitaiki Plains before descending to Lake Taupo. Notable stops include the Te Pohue historic accommodation house, Blowhard Bush scenic reserve for native bird life, and the Tarawera Mill area. The drive takes about two hours without stops. Winter conditions can include fog and ice at altitude, so check conditions with NZTA before departing.

reviews.summary: A surprisingly scenic and dramatic drive connecting two popular regions. The Titiokura Summit viewpoint is outstanding. The road quality is good but requires attention on the winding sections.

pois.name: Napier Art Deco Quarter
pois.short: One of the world's finest collections of Art Deco architecture, rebuilt after the devastating 1931 earthquake.
pois.commentary: Napier's Art Deco Quarter is internationally recognised as one of the world's best-preserved collections of 1930s Art Deco architecture. Following the devastating 7.8-magnitude earthquake on 3 February 1931, which killed 256 people and levelled the city, Napier was rebuilt rapidly in the prevailing architectural styles of the day: Art Deco, Spanish Mission, and Stripped Classical. The result is an extraordinarily cohesive streetscape of geometric facades, decorative motifs, and pastel colours that has been carefully preserved. The Art Deco Trust offers daily guided walking tours that bring the earthquake story and architectural details to life. Self-guided walks with maps from the Trust are also available. The annual Art Deco Weekend each February draws thousands of visitors who dress in period costume for vintage car parades, jazz performances, and themed events. The Marine Parade along the seafront adds palm-lined elegance. A UNESCO World Heritage nomination is under consideration.

reviews.summary: A truly unique architectural heritage experience. The guided walks are informative and the earthquake story is powerful. The Art Deco Weekend festival is a spectacle worth planning around.

pois.name: Abbey Estate Winery
pois.short: Family-owned Hawke's Bay winery with cellar door tastings and vineyard dining.
pois.commentary: Abbey Estate is a family-owned boutique winery on the Gimblett Gravels, Hawke's Bay's most celebrated wine subregion. The gravelly soils and warm microclimate produce exceptional reds, particularly Syrah and Cabernet blends. The cellar door offers tastings in a relaxed setting with views across the vineyard to the Ngaruroro River. The estate's restaurant serves seasonal dishes designed to pair with the wines. Abbey Estate is less well-known than the bigger Hawke's Bay names, making it a more intimate cellar door experience without the crowds. The Gimblett Gravels area was once a river bed, and the stony soils that challenged early farmers have proved ideal for viticulture. Booking recommended for restaurant dining.

reviews.summary: A lovely boutique winery with excellent wines and friendly service. The Gimblett Gravels location is special. Less crowded than the big-name estates.

pois.name: Napier Art Deco Quarter
pois.short: The Art Deco capital of the world — rebuilt in the 1930s style after the devastating 1931 earthquake.
pois.commentary: On 3 February 1931, a 7.8 magnitude earthquake devastated Napier. The rebuild created one of the most concentrated collections of Art Deco architecture in the world.

reviews.summary: A stunning collection of Art Deco architecture — genuinely world-class. The earthquake story is sobering and compelling.

pois.name: MTG Hawke's Bay Museum
pois.short: Museum, Theatre, Gallery complex — Hawke's Bay's premier cultural institution.
pois.commentary: MTG Hawke's Bay houses a significant collection of Māori taonga, a powerful earthquake exhibition, and rotating art shows.

reviews.summary: The earthquake exhibition is powerful and moving. Māori taonga collection is excellent.

pois.name: Te Mata Peak
pois.short: Iconic summit with 360° views — Māori legend says it's the body of a sleeping giant.
pois.commentary: Te Mata Peak rises 399 metres above the Heretaunga Plains, offering 360-degree views.

reviews.summary: Spectacular views — one of the best easily-accessible viewpoints in NZ. Sunset from the summit is unforgettable.

pois.name: Cape Kidnappers Gannet Colony
pois.short: The world's largest mainland gannet colony — thousands of birds nesting on dramatic coastal cliffs.
pois.commentary: Cape Kidnappers is home to the world's largest accessible mainland gannet colony, with around 20,000 gannets.

reviews.summary: An extraordinary wildlife experience — the sheer number of birds is overwhelming.

pois.name: National Aquarium of New Zealand
pois.short: NZ's only public aquarium — kiwi encounter, tuatara, sharks, and marine wildlife.
pois.commentary: The National Aquarium of New Zealand in Napier is the country's only public aquarium.

reviews.summary: The kiwi encounter alone is worth the visit. Shark tank tunnel is impressive.

pois.name: Birdwoods Gallery & Sweet Shop
pois.short: Charming art gallery and old-fashioned sweet shop in heritage grounds near Havelock North.
pois.commentary: Birdwoods combines an art gallery, old-fashioned sweet shop, and garden café in heritage grounds.

reviews.summary: The sweet shop is a delight for kids and adults. Gallery has quality NZ art.

pois.name: Ōtātara Pā Historic Reserve
pois.short: One of NZ's largest pā sites — terraces and ditches of ancient Māori fortification with commanding views.
pois.commentary: Ōtātara Pā is one of the largest and best-preserved pā sites in New Zealand.

reviews.summary: Impressive earthworks that bring pre-European Māori life to life. Commanding views over the Heretaunga Plains.

pois.name: Napier Urban Winery Precinct
pois.short: Walkable cluster of urban wineries, craft breweries, and restaurants in central Napier.
pois.commentary: Central Napier has developed a walkable cluster of urban wineries and craft breweries.

reviews.summary: Great concept — wine tasting without driving. Good combination of wine, beer, and food options.

pois.name: Shine Falls / Te Paripari
pois.short: Hawke's Bay's tallest waterfall at 58 metres — a beautiful bush walk through farmland and native forest.
pois.commentary: Shine Falls drops 58 metres — it's Hawke's Bay's tallest waterfall and one of the most impressive in the North Island.

reviews.summary: Impressive waterfall — the 58m drop is dramatic. Best after rain for maximum water flow.

pois.name: Havelock North Village Arts Precinct
pois.short: The cultural heart of Hawke's Bay wine country — a compact village precinct packed with galleries, artisan studios, and design stores.
pois.commentary: Havelock North sits below Te Mata Peak and has cultivated one of the most concentrated arts precincts outside Wellington. Medici Gallery, Findex Art, and a rotating cast of artisan studios share the village with excellent cafes including the long-standing Rush Munro's ice cream parlour — a Hawke's Bay institution since 1926. The village is the natural base for Craggy Range, Black Barn, and Elephant Hill wineries. A Saturday morning stroll here followed by a winery lunch is among the best half-days in the Bay.

reviews.summary: Visitors rate Havelock North as one of NZ's most charming small towns. The gallery and café mix is well-curated. Rush Munro's ice cream gets special mentions for its heritage and quality.

pois.name: Napier Prison (He Whare Herehere o Napier)
pois.short: New Zealand's oldest operating prison — now a heritage tourism site with daytime and ghost tours.
pois.commentary: Built in 1862 from locally quarried limestone, Napier Prison operated continuously until 1993 and held some of New Zealand's most notorious criminals. The site survived the 1931 earthquake largely intact — a rare pre-quake building in a city otherwise rebuilt entirely in Art Deco. Today it offers guided heritage tours exploring the colonial prison system, solitary confinement cells, and execution chamber. Evening ghost tours are extremely popular and sell out weeks ahead in summer. The site gives candid, unglamourised accounts of New Zealand's prison history and the limestone architecture is striking throughout.

reviews.summary: A compelling and well-run heritage attraction. Ghost tours are immersive and genuinely unsettling. Daytime tours give excellent historical context on colonial-era justice.

pois.name: Toitoi — Hawke's Bay Arts and Events Centre, Hastings
pois.short: Hastings' major performing arts and events venue — concerts, theatre, and community events year-round.
pois.commentary: Toitoi opened in 2020 as Hastings' centrepiece performing arts venue, combining a 1,250-seat auditorium with flexible gallery and function spaces. The building was designed with strong connection to the region's Arts and Crafts and Art Deco architectural heritage. Programming spans NZ Symphony Orchestra visits, theatre touring productions, comedy shows, and community events including the Hawke's Bay Arts Festival. The venue has helped reinvigorate Hastings' cultural scene alongside the Hawke's Bay Museum. Check the programme before visiting — the venue is at its best during a live performance.

reviews.summary: A genuinely world-class regional venue. The acoustics and design are highly praised. Locals are proud of it and the programming is ambitious for a city of Hastings' size.

pois.name: Hawke's Bay Farmers' Market, Hastings Showgrounds
pois.short: One of New Zealand's finest farmers' markets — every Sunday morning at the Hawke's Bay Showgrounds.
pois.commentary: The Hawke's Bay Farmers' Market runs every Sunday morning at the Showgrounds and consistently rates among NZ's top three regional markets. The horticultural bounty of the Heretaunga Plains makes itself obvious here: stone fruit in summer, apples and pears in autumn, citrus through winter, and year-round olive oil, artisan cheese, butchers, bakers, and coffee roasters. Over 60 stalls pack the showgrounds. Arrive before 9am for the best selection — popular stalls sell out. The market has been running since 2000 and has a devoted local following.

reviews.summary: Rated one of New Zealand's finest farmers' markets. The quality of produce reflects the exceptional Heretaunga growing region. The atmosphere is warm and community-focused.

pois.name: Kaweka Forest Park — Boundary Stream and Ridge Tracks
pois.short: The rugged Kaweka Range rises directly behind Napier — offering serious tramping and stunning views across Hawke's Bay.
pois.commentary: The Kaweka Forest Park is one of the largest areas of native forest in the North Island and sits directly behind Napier. DOC maintains a network of tracks and huts through the range; the Makino to Kaweka J Hut circuit is a demanding two-day tramp through subalpine tussock and native beech. Day walkers can access the range via Boundary Stream Track (flat, kahikatea forest, excellent birding) or steeper tracks from Te Puia Springs Road. The Kaweka J summit at 1,724m gives panoramic views from the Pacific to the Central Plateau on clear days. The range also sees regular hunting of red deer and wild pigs.

reviews.summary: A tramping destination that punches above its profile. The remote feel is genuine, just minutes from Napier. Birding at Boundary Stream is excellent.

pois.name: Te Mata Peak Mountain Bike Trails
pois.short: Te Mata Peak Park's expanding mountain bike network — gravity trails from the summit with epic Hawke's Bay views.
pois.commentary: Te Mata Peak Park has developed mountain bike trails over the past decade, now offering a range of trails from smooth green-grade loops to technical black-grade descents from the summit. The Te Mata Trust manages the park in partnership with the Hawke's Bay Mountain Bike Club. The summit is accessible by road, making descent-focused riding accessible to a wide range of riders — pedal or drive up, and gravity does the rest. The views from the ridge across the Heretaunga Plains and out to the Pacific are a constant backdrop. The trails integrate with the park's walking network, so early morning riding before walkers arrive makes for an ideal experience.

reviews.summary: A growing network with great views. The green and blue grade trails are well-maintained and suit intermediate riders. The gravity-fed descents are a real selling point.

pois.name: Ngaruroro River Kayaking and Packrafting
pois.short: The Ngaruroro River offers accessible day and multi-day kayaking through Hawke's Bay wine country and gorge country.
pois.commentary: The Ngaruroro begins in the Kaweka and Ruahine ranges and descends through gorge country before spreading across the Heretaunga Plains. The lower river sections — from around Fernhill to the estuary — offer easy grade 1–2 paddling through willow-lined channels past vineyards and orchards. Guided packraft trips explore the upper river where flows are more dynamic. The Ngaruroro is less well-known than the Mohaka but offers a more accessible, social paddling experience and the chance to combine river time with winery visits at the end of the day. The Hawke's Bay Trails network also runs through the river corridor.
sources_to_check: Hawke's Bay Regional Council waterway access maps

reviews.summary: A peaceful and scenic river experience through wine country. Best in summer when water levels suit beginners. The natural combination with nearby wineries makes for a perfect day out.

pois.name: Tandem Skydiving — Clive Aerodrome, Hawke's Bay
pois.short: Freefall over Hawke's Bay's coastline with views from Cape Kidnappers to the Kaweka Range.
pois.commentary: Skydive Hawke's Bay operates from Clive Aerodrome south of Napier on the Heretaunga Plains. The drop zone gives freefall over an extraordinary panorama — the curved coastline from Cape Kidnappers south to the Bay of Plenty north, the patchwork of vineyards across the plains, and the Kaweka and Ruahine ranges rising to the west. Tandem jumps from 9,000, 12,000, and 15,000 feet are available; the 15,000-foot option gives over a minute of freefall. The coastal setting and clear-weather frequency make Hawke's Bay one of the more reliably available skydive locations in the North Island.

reviews.summary: The coastal and vineyard scenery makes this one of NZ's most visually spectacular skydive locations. Professional operation with experienced tandem masters. Video packages are worth it.

pois.name: Ocean Beach Surfing, Hawke's Bay
pois.short: A long, remote surf beach 20km east of Havelock North — open ocean swell and no crowds.
pois.commentary: Ocean Beach is one of Hawke's Bay's longest stretches of uninterrupted shoreline — over 15km of gravel-sand beach receiving open Pacific swell. Accessed via a winding road through the Black Barn and Craggy Range wine country, the relative difficulty of access keeps it uncrowded. Surf conditions range from small rolling swells to solid overhead sets during easterly and southerly weather systems. No lifeguards operate and rips can be strong — experienced surfers only in bigger conditions. The route out passes several wineries, making a post-session cellar door visit a natural combination.

reviews.summary: A beautiful remote beach with reliable surf. The access road through wine country is half the experience. Best for intermediate-advanced surfers in larger conditions.

pois.name: Cape Kidnappers Clifftop Walk (Self-Guided)
pois.short: An 8km clifftop walk to the world's most accessible mainland gannet colony — dramatic coastal scenery throughout.
pois.commentary: The self-guided clifftop walk to Cape Kidnappers is the longest and most spectacular way to reach the gannet colony. Starting from Clifton Reserve, the track climbs onto coastal cliffs and traverses narrow ridgelines with sheer drops to the sea. The colony — up to 20,000 Australasian gannets nesting at the tip of the cape — is one of the world's largest mainland gannet colonies, visible from late October through April. The round trip is approximately 16km and takes 6–7 hours. The walk is tidal and time-restricted — DOC issues detailed tide-table guidance. A guided tractor-trailer service along the beach is a less strenuous alternative.

reviews.summary: One of the great coastal walks in the North Island. The gannet colony is extraordinary. Tide timing is critical — plan carefully. Worth every step.

pois.name: Cape Kidnappers Gannet Colony
pois.short: World's largest mainland gannet colony on dramatic coastal cliffs.
pois.commentary: Cape Kidnappers hosts the world's largest and most accessible mainland gannet colony, with around 20,000 birds. The colony is active from September to April. The 19-kilometre return beach walk must be timed with tides. Guided tractor tours and 4WD tours offer easier access from the inland side. The gannets are remarkably tolerant of visitors.

reviews.summary: An extraordinary wildlife spectacle. The gannets are fearless and photogenic.

pois.name: Napier Art Deco Quarter
pois.short: One of the world's finest concentrations of Art Deco architecture, rebuilt after the 1931 earthquake.
pois.commentary: Napier's Art Deco Quarter is one of the world's most complete collections of Art Deco architecture. Self-guided and guided walking tours take in dozens of beautifully maintained buildings. The Art Deco Centre runs daily guided walks and hosts the annual Art Deco Festival in February. The adjacent Ahuriri village has waterfront dining.

reviews.summary: A genuinely unique architectural experience. The guided walks bring the buildings to life.

pois.name: Mission Estate Winery
pois.short: New Zealand's oldest winery, established 1851, with heritage buildings and summer concerts.
pois.commentary: Mission Estate Winery is New Zealand's oldest winery, established by French Marist missionaries in 1851 and continuously operating ever since. The winery occupies a gracious heritage complex on Church Road in Napier, with the original seminary building now housing the cellar door and restaurant. The wines, particularly the Jewelstone range of Syrah and Chardonnay, have won international awards. The Mission Estate Concert Series each summer has hosted international acts including Rod Stewart, Elton John, and Sting on the beautiful hillside amphitheatre. The restaurant serves Hawke's Bay produce with vineyard views. Free cellar door tastings run daily. The winery's unbroken 170-year history makes it a unique piece of New Zealand's cultural heritage.

reviews.summary: Beautiful heritage setting with good wines. The summer concerts are legendary. Cellar door tastings are free and generous.

pois.name: Cape Kidnappers Golf Course
pois.short: World-ranked clifftop golf course with dramatic ocean views.
pois.commentary: Cape Kidnappers Golf Course is consistently ranked among the top 20 golf courses in the world, designed by Tom Doak on a dramatic clifftop property overlooking the Pacific Ocean. The course sits 140 metres above the sea on finger-like ridges between deep ravines, creating some of the most spectacular (and terrifying) golf holes anywhere. Several tee shots require carries over deep chasms with ocean views. The course is part of the luxury Cape Kidnappers resort and is open to visiting golfers, though green fees reflect its world-class status. The drive to the course through working farmland passes the gannet colony access point. Caddies are recommended for first-time players to navigate the dramatic layout.

reviews.summary: Golf at its most dramatic. The cliff-edge holes are genuinely vertigo-inducing. World-class in every respect. Not cheap but unforgettable.

pois.name: Tuki Tuki Valley Wine Trail
pois.short: Scenic wine cycling trail through Hawke's Bay's premium wine sub-region.
pois.commentary: The Tuki Tuki Valley south of Havelock North is one of Hawke's Bay's premium wine sub-regions, and the flat valley roads make it ideal for cycling between cellar doors. The valley is flanked by Te Mata Peak on one side and the Tukituki River on the other, creating a sheltered microclimate perfect for Bordeaux-style reds and aromatic whites. Key wineries include Craggy Range, Te Mata Estate, Trinity Hill, and Black Barn, each offering distinctive cellar door experiences. Several bike hire operators provide quality bikes and maps. The cycling route is flat and largely traffic-free. Black Barn hosts regular outdoor cinema and farmers market events that have become Hawke's Bay institutions. The valley's beauty and accessibility make it the most popular wine cycling area outside Marlborough.

reviews.summary: The best way to experience Hawke's Bay wine country. Flat, easy cycling between world-class wineries. Te Mata Peak backdrop is stunning.

pois.name: Hastings Municipal Theatre / Opera House
pois.short: Beautifully restored 1915 theatre rebuilt in Spanish Mission style after the 1931 earthquake.
pois.commentary: The Hastings Municipal Theatre is a beautifully maintained 1915 theatre that was rebuilt in Spanish Mission style after the 1931 earthquake, making it one of the few surviving examples of this architectural style in New Zealand. The ornate interior features decorative plasterwork, a balcony, and excellent acoustics. The theatre hosts a year-round programme of professional and amateur productions, concerts, and community events. Heritage tours are available and the enthusiastic guides explain the building's earthquake story and architectural significance. The theatre sits in Hastings' civic precinct alongside other earthquake-era buildings. Combined with Napier's Art Deco, it illustrates the diversity of 1930s rebuilding styles adopted in the region.

reviews.summary: A beautiful heritage theatre with real character. The Spanish Mission architecture is distinctive. Active programme of events.

pois.name: Napier Art Deco Quarter
pois.short: One of the world's finest collections of Art Deco architecture, rebuilt after the 1931 earthquake.
pois.commentary: Napier's Art Deco Quarter is one of the world's most complete and concentrated collections of Art Deco architecture, born from tragedy — the devastating 1931 Hawke's Bay earthquake that killed 256 people and destroyed the town centre. The rebuilt Napier, constructed rapidly in the prevailing Art Deco style of the early 1930s, is now a globally significant architectural heritage site. The Art Deco Trust offers guided walking tours that bring the buildings to life with stories of the earthquake, the rebuild, and the architectural details. The annual Art Deco Festival (February) sees the entire city dress in period costume. Self-guided walk maps are available from the Art Deco Centre.

reviews.summary: A world-class heritage experience. The guided walks add tremendous value. The earthquake story is gripping.

pois.name: Bridge Pa Wine Triangle
pois.short: Cluster of boutique wineries in Hawke's Bay's premium red wine sub-region.
pois.commentary: The Bridge Pa Wine Triangle is a compact cluster of boutique wineries in Hawke's Bay's warmest sub-region, producing some of New Zealand's finest Syrah, Cabernet Sauvignon, and Merlot. The Triangle — roughly bounded by Bridge Pa, Ngatarawa, and Roy's Hill — contains over a dozen cellar doors within cycling distance of each other, making it perfect for a self-guided wine tour by bike. The heavy, free-draining gravel soils and warm microclimate produce concentrated, complex red wines. Highlights include Ngatarawa, Alpha Domus, and Trinity Hill. The cycling option (bikes available for hire in Hastings) removes the drink-driving question and adds to the experience.

reviews.summary: Outstanding boutique wineries close together. Cycling between them is ideal. The reds are superb.

pois.name: Te Mata Estate Winery
pois.short: Historic Hawke's Bay winery with acclaimed wines and views beneath Te Mata Peak.
pois.commentary: Te Mata Estate is one of New Zealand's oldest and most respected wineries, established in 1896 and producing wines that consistently rank among the country's finest. Their Coleraine Cabernet/Merlot blend is regarded as one of New Zealand's greatest red wines. The cellar door, beneath the limestone bluffs of Te Mata Peak, offers tastings in a heritage setting with views across the Heretaunga Plains. The estate's history spans over 125 years, and the winery buildings reflect this heritage. Wine tours and tastings provide insight into why Hawke's Bay has become New Zealand's premier red wine region, with the warm climate and ancient gravel soils producing wines of exceptional depth and complexity.

reviews.summary: Outstanding wines from one of NZ's oldest estates. The Coleraine is legendary. Beautiful heritage setting beneath Te Mata Peak.

pois.name: Ocean Beach & Waimarama Coast
pois.short: Wild, beautiful beach on Hawke's Bay's southern coast — uncrowded and spectacular.
pois.commentary: Ocean Beach and the Waimarama coastline south of Hastings offer some of Hawke's Bay's most beautiful and uncrowded beach scenery. Ocean Beach is a long, dramatic stretch of dark sand backed by eroded clay cliffs and farmland, accessible via a steep road from the village. The swimming can be rough — this is a surf beach exposed to Pacific swells — but the scenery is spectacular. Neighbouring Waimarama Beach is more sheltered and family-friendly. Bare Island, accessible on foot at low tide, adds interest. The drive through rolling pastoral country to reach these beaches is pleasant, and the contrast with the manicured wine country inland is refreshing.

reviews.summary: Wild, beautiful beach scenery away from the wine-country crowds. Dramatic cliffs and surf. Waimarama is good for families.

pois.name: Tukituki Trail — Cycling
pois.short: Scenic cycling trail following the Tukituki River from inland Hawke's Bay to the coast.
pois.commentary: The Tukituki Trail is a scenic cycling route following the Tukituki River from the inland valleys of Central Hawke's Bay to the coast near Cape Kidnappers. The trail passes through farmland, orchards, and vineyards with Te Mata Peak as a constant landmark. The route is mostly flat or gently graded, making it accessible to casual cyclists. The landscape transitions from productive farmland through limestone country to the dramatic coastal cliffs near the river mouth. Bike hire is available in Havelock North. The trail can be combined with wine tasting stops and is part of the wider Hawke's Bay Trails network. The Tukituki River itself is excellent for swimming in the warmer months.

reviews.summary: Pleasant cycling through beautiful pastoral landscape. Te Mata Peak backdrop is dramatic. Good swimming spots along the way.

pois.name: Cape Kidnappers Gannet Colony / Te Kauwae-a-Māui
pois.short: One of the world's largest mainland gannet colonies with 25,000+ birds.
pois.commentary: Cape Kidnappers hosts the largest and most accessible mainland gannet colony in the world, with over 25,000 Australasian gannets nesting on dramatic coastal cliffs between September and April. Access is via a spectacular 19km return beach and clifftop walk from Clifton (tide-dependent), or by guided tractor-trailer or 4WD tours along the beach. The birds are remarkably unafraid of humans, allowing incredibly close-up observation of their elaborate courtship displays, nest-building, and chick-rearing. The cape itself is named after an incident during Captain Cook's 1769 visit. The walk along the beach beneath towering clay cliffs is dramatic in its own right. Check tide times carefully — the beach is impassable at high tide.

reviews.summary: The sheer number of gannets and the close-up access are incredible. The coastal scenery is stunning. Visitors strongly recommend checking tide times and starting early.

pois.name: Craggy Range Winery
pois.short: World-class winery at the foot of Te Mata Peak with acclaimed restaurant.
pois.commentary: Craggy Range is one of New Zealand's most acclaimed wineries, dramatically situated at the foot of Te Mata Peak in Havelock North. The winery produces exceptional single-vineyard wines from sites across Hawke's Bay and Martinborough, with their Te Muna Road Pinot Noir and Sophia Gimblett Gravels red blend consistently ranked among NZ's finest. The cellar door offers tastings and behind-the-scenes winery tours, while the on-site restaurant serves a sophisticated menu matched to the wines. The terrace dining area has views directly up to Te Mata Peak. Architecture and landscaping are first-rate, reflecting the winery's commitment to excellence in every detail. Bookings recommended for the restaurant.

reviews.summary: Wine connoisseurs rate the wines among NZ's best. The restaurant is outstanding, and the Te Mata Peak setting is spectacular. A world-class winery experience.

pois.name: Napier Art Deco Heritage Walking Tour
pois.short: Walk through the world's most concentrated collection of Art Deco buildings — rebuilt after the devastating 1931 earthquake.
pois.commentary: Napier was devastated by a magnitude 7.8 earthquake on 3 February 1931, which killed 256 people and destroyed the central business district. The city was rebuilt rapidly in the prevailing Art Deco style of the era, creating what is now the world's most concentrated collection of Art Deco architecture. Guided walking tours (daily from the Art Deco Centre) explain the buildings' stories, architectural features, and the earthquake that necessitated them. The Māori motifs incorporated by architect Louis Hay are particularly notable. Self-guided walks with audio are also available. The Daily Telegraph building, the Municipal Theatre, and the dome of the former Fire Station are highlights.

reviews.summary: The guided tours are excellent and bring the architecture to life. The earthquake history is moving. The Art Deco buildings are beautifully maintained. Self-guided options work well too.

pois.name: Ocean Beach (Waimārama) Wild Coast Walk
pois.short: A wild, wind-swept beach south of Hastings — kilometres of sand backed by dunes and farmland, with barely another soul.
pois.commentary: Ocean Beach stretches south from Waimārama along Hawke's Bay's coast, a vast sweep of sand pounded by Pacific surf and backed by dunes and pastoral farmland. The beach is popular with locals for walking, horse riding and fishing but rarely busy — walk 15 minutes in either direction and you'll likely have the coast to yourself. The swimming can be hazardous due to rips, so the beach is better for walking and beachcombing. On clear days the views north to Cape Kidnappers are excellent. The light here is particularly beautiful in the golden hour before sunset.

reviews.summary: A beautiful, wild beach perfect for long walks. Rarely crowded. Not ideal for swimming due to strong currents. The views to Cape Kidnappers are excellent. Bring sun protection — no shade.

pois.name: Hawke's Bay Winery Cycle Trail
pois.short: Cycle between cellar doors on flat, vineyard-lined trails — one of New Zealand's best ways to combine wine tasting with exercise.
pois.commentary: The Hawke's Bay wine region is perfectly suited to cycling — flat terrain, warm climate, and cellar doors conveniently spaced along dedicated cycle trails. Several loop options connect top wineries including Mission Estate (NZ's oldest winery), Church Road, Black Barn and dozens of boutique producers. Bike hire is widely available and many companies offer guided tours with support vehicles. The Landscapes Ride and the Water Ride are the most popular routes, weaving through vineyards, orchards and olive groves. It's a civilised way to taste your way through one of New Zealand's finest wine regions without worrying about driving.

reviews.summary: The perfect way to explore the wine region. Flat terrain makes it easy. Many excellent cellar doors to visit. Guided tours handle logistics beautifully. Book bike hire ahead in peak season.

pois.name: Boundary Stream Mainland Island — Kōkako Trail
pois.short: A DOC-managed 'mainland island' in the Kaweka Range where intensive predator control has brought kōkako and other rare birds back from the brink.
pois.commentary: Boundary Stream Mainland Island is a 800-hectare area of native forest in the Kaweka Range where intensive pest control has produced remarkable results. The North Island kōkako — a striking blue-wattled bird with a haunting organ-like call — has been reintroduced and is breeding successfully. Dawn visits offer the best chance of hearing or seeing kōkako, along with North Island robin, whitehead, and falcon. Several walking tracks explore the forest, from short loops to longer tramping routes. The Shine Falls track leads to an impressive 58-metre waterfall. It's a powerful demonstration of what predator control can achieve for native wildlife.

reviews.summary: An excellent birding destination. Dawn is best for kōkako. The forest is beautiful and well-maintained. Shine Falls is a bonus attraction. Some tracks are muddy after rain.

pois.name: Te Mata Peak via Redwoods Track
pois.short: Alternative route to Te Mata Peak through towering California redwood groves.
pois.commentary: While most visitors drive to the summit, the Redwoods Track offers a walking approach through impressive California redwood groves planted in the 1920s. The trail climbs through native bush and exotic forest to emerge at the iconic ridge with panoramic views over Hawke's Bay.

reviews.summary: Far better than driving — the redwoods add a magical dimension.

pois.name: Central Hawke's Bay Heritage Trail
pois.short: Self-drive heritage trail through the historic small towns of Central Hawke's Bay.
pois.commentary: This self-drive trail connects Waipawa, Waipukurau, and surrounding villages through some of New Zealand's earliest European pastoral country. Historic homesteads, churches, and woolsheds tell the story of the region's farming heritage, with stops at artisan food producers along the way.

reviews.summary: Charming rural heritage drive through authentic small-town NZ.

pois.name: Onga Onga Historic Village
pois.short: Tiny heritage village preserving colonial-era buildings in rural Hawke's Bay.
pois.commentary: The hamlet of Onga Onga preserves a remarkable collection of colonial-era buildings including a church, post office, and general store. The small museum and heritage precinct offer an authentic glimpse of 1880s rural New Zealand life, virtually unchanged since settlement.

reviews.summary: A genuine step back in time — tiny but charming.

pois.name: Hawke's Bay Wine Trail by Bike
pois.short: Flat cycling trail linking a dozen cellar doors across the Heretaunga Plains.
pois.commentary: Hawke's Bay's flat terrain makes it ideal for cycling between wineries. The trail network connects over a dozen cellar doors from Havelock North to the coast, passing through orchards and vineyards with stops at Mission Estate (NZ's oldest winery), Craggy Range, and boutique producers.

reviews.summary: Perfect wine region for cycling — flat, scenic, and delicious.

pois.name: Ocean Beach — Waimarama Coast
pois.short: Wild and remote surf beach on Hawke's Bay's dramatic southern coastline.
pois.commentary: Ocean Beach is Hawke's Bay's wild southern coast — a long stretch of black sand backed by dramatic papa cliffs and rolling farmland. Popular with surfers and horse riders, it feels a world away from the manicured wineries just 30 minutes inland.

reviews.summary: Dramatic wild coast — the papa cliffs are spectacular.

pois.name: Silky Oak Chocolate Company
pois.short: Artisan chocolatier hand-making Belgian-style chocolates in Hawke's Bay.
pois.commentary: Silky Oak Chocolate Company crafts premium handmade chocolates using Belgian techniques. Their factory shop offers tastings and chocolate-making demonstrations, with flavours incorporating local ingredients like Hawke's Bay orchard fruits and mānuka honey.

reviews.summary: Exquisite handmade chocolates — the tastings are generous.

pois.name: Craggy Range Winery & Restaurant
pois.short: Acclaimed winery with fine dining beneath Te Mata Peak.
pois.commentary: Craggy Range is consistently rated among New Zealand's top wineries, producing outstanding Syrah and Chardonnay from single vineyards. Their restaurant serves seasonal menus paired with the wines, all set beneath the dramatic backdrop of Te Mata Peak.

reviews.summary: World-class wine and dining — the Te Mata Peak setting is breathtaking.
